title: Steps to Distance Calculator — km, Miles, Calories & Time
shortDescription: Convert step count to distance in km or miles. Estimates walking time and calories burned based on your height, stride length, and weight.
description: Enter your step count along with your height (to auto-estimate stride length) or a custom stride length, and instantly see how far you walked in kilometers or miles, how long it took, and an estimate of the calories you burned. Supports both metric and imperial units.
metaDescription: Convert steps to distance in km or miles. Estimates calories burned and walking time from step count, height, and weight. Free, no sign-up.
empty: Enter your step count and height above to calculate distance, time, and calories.

sections.heading: The 10,000-step target: where it came from and what the research actually says
paragraphs: The idea that 10,000 steps a day is the magic number for good health originated not from a clinical trial but from a Japanese marketing campaign. In 1965, a company called Yamasa marketed one of the world's first pedometers under the name '万歩計' (Manpokei), which literally means '10,000-step meter'. The goal was a round, memorable number — not a scientifically derived threshold.

Modern research has since examined the claim rigorously. A large-scale study published in JAMA Internal Medicine (Lee et al., 2019) found that among older women, mortality risk dropped significantly as daily steps increased from about 2,700 to around 7,500, with no meaningful additional benefit beyond that. A 2021 analysis in JAMA Network Open found that even 7,000 steps per day was associated with substantially lower all-cause mortality compared to fewer steps. The relationship between step count and health outcomes is real and robust — but the threshold is lower than 10,000 for most of the measurable benefit.

For cardiovascular health, the American Heart Association notes that any increase in daily movement is beneficial. Going from 4,000 to 6,000 steps is proportionally more impactful than going from 8,000 to 10,000. The practical takeaway: don't let perfect be the enemy of good. If you currently average 4,000 steps, aiming for 6,000 is a highly effective health intervention.

sections.heading: How stride length is calculated and why it varies
paragraphs: Stride length (the distance covered in a single step, heel to heel of the same foot) correlates strongly with height. The widely used estimate is that stride length equals approximately 41.5% of height for men and 41.3% for women. These ratios come from biomechanics research and are reliable averages — but individual variation is substantial. Leg-to-height ratio, walking speed, footwear, surface incline, age, and gait style all affect the actual distance per step.

Walking speed has a particularly large effect: at a brisk pace (about 5–6 km/h), stride length increases by 15–20% compared to a slow stroll (3 km/h). Running strides are 50–100% longer than walking strides at the same cadence. This tool uses a fixed estimate appropriate for moderate walking pace. If you have measured your actual stride length — for example, by counting steps over a known distance on a track — use the 'custom stride length' mode for more accurate results.

Age is another factor: stride length decreases by roughly 0.5–1% per decade after age 40 due to changes in muscle strength, flexibility, and balance. Older adults who exercise regularly tend to maintain stride length better than sedentary peers. Footwear can add 2–4 cm of apparent stride length due to heel-to-toe transitions in modern running shoes, while barefoot walking tends to produce shorter, flatter strides.

sections.heading: Calorie estimation from step count: methodology and limitations
paragraphs: This calculator uses the MET (Metabolic Equivalent of Task) framework to estimate calorie burn. Walking at a moderate pace has an MET of approximately 3.5, meaning the activity burns 3.5 times as many calories per unit time as sitting quietly. The formula is: calories = MET × body weight (kg) × duration (hours). Duration is estimated by dividing your step count by a cadence of 100 steps per minute, which is the typical pace for moderate-intensity walking.

Weight is the dominant variable in calorie estimation. A 90 kg person burns roughly 50% more calories walking the same distance as a 60 kg person, because more mass must be moved against gravity. If you leave the weight field blank, the calculator uses a default of 70 kg — a reasonable adult average but likely inaccurate for your specific case. For a more accurate calorie estimate, enter your actual weight.

Bear in mind that the MET method produces estimates with typical accuracy of ±15–20%. Fitness trackers with heart rate monitors tend to outperform MET-based estimates because they respond to individual metabolic variation. However, for planning purposes — deciding whether a walking goal will meaningfully contribute to a calorie deficit — MET-based estimates are entirely adequate and well-validated in the exercise science literature.

howTo.name: Enter your step count
howTo.text: Type the number of steps from your pedometer, phone health app, or fitness tracker into the 'Number of steps' field.

howTo.name: Set your unit system and gender
howTo.text: Choose metric (cm/km/kg) or imperial (in/mi/lb). Select your gender — this affects the stride-length multiplier used in the height-based estimate.

howTo.name: Provide your height or custom stride
howTo.text: Leave 'Estimate from height' selected and enter your height for an automatic stride estimate. Or switch to 'Custom stride length' and enter a measured value if you know it from a track or treadmill test.

howTo.name: Optionally enter your weight
howTo.text: Adding your body weight gives a more accurate calorie estimate. The default is 70 kg / 154 lb if left blank.

faq.q: How accurate is the distance estimate?
faq.a: The height-based stride estimate has a typical accuracy of ±10% for average-height adults walking at a moderate pace. Factors like walking speed, terrain, and footwear can push the error higher. For precision (e.g., training distances), measure your actual stride on a track and use custom mode.

faq.q: What stride multiplier does the calculator use?
faq.a: The tool uses 0.415 × height for men, 0.413 × height for women, and 0.414 for 'Other'. These ratios are derived from biomechanics research and represent a comfortable moderate walking pace.

Time is estimated at 100 steps per minute, the typical cadence for a moderate-intensity walk. A brisk walk might be 110–120 steps/min; a casual stroll around 90 steps/min. The estimate will be slightly high for brisk walkers and slightly low for slow walkers.

faq.q: Why does weight affect calorie count?
faq.a: Heavier bodies require more energy to move the same distance against gravity. The calorie formula scales linearly with body weight, so a 100 kg person burns roughly 43% more calories for the same walk as a 70 kg person.

faq.q: What does 'stride length' mean — is it one step or two?
faq.a: In this calculator, stride length is the distance of a single step (one foot to the other foot). Some sources define 'stride' as two steps (one full gait cycle). The formulas used here are based on single-step distance as a proportion of height.
For someone with a 75 cm stride, 1 km = 1,000 ÷ 0.75 = approximately 1,333 steps. For a shorter 65 cm stride, it's about 1,538 steps. The exact number depends heavily on stride length.
One mile is 1,609 meters. At a typical 75 cm stride, that's about 2,145 steps. Most health references use a round figure of 2,000 steps per mile as a rough average across heights.

faq.q: Can I use this for running instead of walking?
faq.a: Running stride lengths are 60–100% longer than walking strides, and running cadence is also faster. If you're tracking a run, measure your actual running stride on a track and use the 'custom stride length' mode. The calorie estimate will also be significantly low for running (MET for running is 7–10, not 3.5).
